_provenance: fan-out:wf_d565ece0 (haiku research + sonnet verify + sonnet score); enriched 2026-06-20 with deep web research (10+ sources, engine correction Volvo D3, furler failure detail, full spec/SA-D data)
name: Amel 54

facts.value: Class A - Extended oceanic voyages, wind force exceeding 8 (Beaufort scale), significant wave heights exceeding 4m, excluding abnormal conditions; vessels largely self-sufficient

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: 1.9 (less than 2.0 threshold, indicating suitability for ocean passages)

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: Bulb on an L-shaped fin keel with a fixed ballast ratio of 29% (ballast approximately 5,100 kg on a 17,500 kg displacement); single rudder hung on a full-length skeg. Keel material is most often described as cast iron but is not confirmed by a primary builder source.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: Hull length 16.4m (53'10"), LOA with overhangs 17.2m (56'5"), beam 4.8m (15'8"), draft 2.1m (6'11"), displacement 17,500 kg (38,581 lb). Cruising World quotes a slightly higher 35,000-38,580 lb cruising displacement. Fuel and water are each 900 litres (237 US gal).

Upwind sail area approximately 140 m² (1,507 sq ft). Sail-area-to-displacement ratio is about 21-22, displacement-to-length about 135, comfort ratio about 29 - figures typical of a moderately canvassed, heavy bluewater cruiser that is unhurried in light air but stiff and easily driven offshore. Cruising World logged an effortless 9 knots in 16 knots of beam wind.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: Built by Chantiers Amel (La Rochelle, France) from about 2005 to 2011, roughly 50 hulls, succeeding the Super Maramu and itself replaced by the Amel 55 in 2011. Designed in-house by the Amel design office.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: 110 hp Volvo Penta D3 diesel - one of the first fully electronic common-rail engines Amel used. Brokerage sheets list it generically as a '110 hp Volvo TurboDiesel'; model-specific sources (Amel School, Cruising Consulting) identify it as the Volvo D3. This is a significant correction to earlier records that listed a mechanical TAMD-series engine, which was used on the older Super Maramu but not the 54. Drives an AUTOPROP folding propeller through a hydraulic gearbox.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht
Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

facts.value: The Volvo D3 is widely cited as the 54's weakest design choice. Amel School notes it 'has a reputation for being less reliable than other power plants used by the yard,' and Cruising Consulting writes 'Amel never really found the perfect engine for the 54... an early fully-electronic engine which has been a source of trouble for some owners,' adding that later Volvo updates reduced the issues. Documented failure modes are electronic rather than mechanical: boost-pressure sensor failure (around 420 euros), fault codes dropping the engine into a 1500 rpm limp mode, and diagnosis requiring a Volvo VODIA tester. A diesel-specialist survey is strongly advised before purchase.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht
Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting
Volvo D3 110 hp - Problems? | YBW Forum

facts.value: Webasto 5000W diesel heater confirmed on the 2009 Michel Charpentier listing, distributing warm air into the enclosed doghouse. A 2010 listing specifies an Espar/Eberspacher 5000W unit instead, so the brand varied by hull. The fully enclosed doghouse with a hard windshield is the real cold-weather strength: the crew steers and runs the boat from a heated, sheltered position.

Balsa-cored fiberglass/polyester sandwich deck over a single-skin fiberglass/polyester hull. Construction materials are confirmed, but no source quantifies insulation thickness or cold-weather comfort. The enclosed doghouse and heater matter more for cold passages than hull insulation does.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance indicators

facts.value: Deck-stepped masts: both the main and mizzen are stepped on deck, with massive compression assemblies carrying the loads into the hull structure. Two levels of swept-back spreaders. Not keel-stepped, so there is no mast cold-bridge into the cabin.

Four watertight bulkheads divide the hull into independent compartments, a core Amel safety feature that limits flooding after a collision or grounding. Confirmed by both sailboatdata and the Cruising World review.

Enclosed doghouse helm with push-button controls for almost every system and a seated position behind a hard windshield. Cruising World confirms the controls and readouts but notes visibility from the chair is limited - the classic Amel trade-off of all-weather shelter for sightlines. The boat is genuinely sailed from inside in foul weather.

Natural ventilation through opening deck hatches, portlights and coachroof hatches, supplemented by roughly six fans distributed through the accommodation (per the 2009 listing). The specific hatch count is not confirmed in sources. Flush deck hatches should be checked carefully for leaks per Amel School's survey guidance.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

facts.value: The fully enclosed doghouse with curved windows shades the helm and saloon area and the boat carries twin reaching poles for downwind shade-and-sail rigs, but no source documents dedicated cabin shading such as roller blinds or solar awnings. Treat cabin shade as undocumented rather than absent.

Three separate cold-storage units: a front-loading 24V refrigerator, a top-loading 24V galley freezer, and a second freezer under the saloon settee. Confirmed by the 2009 Charpentier listing and the Cruising World review. Exceptional cold capacity for a short-handed couple provisioning for long passages.

Reverse-osmosis watermaker is standard equipment. The 2009 example carries a Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit. Generic RO pressure and yield figures from earlier records were removed as unsourced. Combined with 900 litres of tankage, this makes the boat genuinely self-sufficient for water on long tropical passages.

Three reversible air-conditioning units (aft cabin, saloon, forward cabin) are standard equipment, confirmed by both the 2009 and 2010 Charpentier listings. AC was designed in, not added afterward, which is why owners run the boat in the tropics in comfort - but it is a heavy electrical and seawater-cooling load that drives generator and battery sizing.

A Hydrovane independent vane-to-auxiliary-rudder system was fitted to at least one Amel 54 (the 2009 Charpentier listing) as a retrofit, giving steering and an emergency rudder with no electrical draw. Not factory standard - the factory self-steering is the autopilot.

Two Furuno Navpilot 511 autopilots are documented on the well-equipped 2009 example, giving full redundancy for short-handed ocean passages. This is the equipment on a specific vessel rather than a guaranteed fit on every hull.

24V house system. The 2009 example carries an Onan 11 kVA generator and a 12 x 110Ah (1,320 Ah) 24V battery bank with a Mastervolt charger and a 2,500W inverter. Exact factory battery capacity varies by hull and refit history, but the heavy hotel load (three AC units, dual refrigeration, watermaker, electric furlers and winches) means generous battery and charging capacity is essential.

Most 54s offer an optional hydraulic passerelle for stern boarding, but it is one of the boat's most criticised features. Cruising Consulting calls it 'very cool and awesome when it works' but with 'a reputation for constant mechanical problems and serious corrosion issues,' adding 'I haven't met anyone who was happy with it.' Budget a rebuild or removal.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting
Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

facts.value: High bulwarks with full-length stainless-steel railings give a notably secure feel on deck, confirmed across sources. The exact freeboard measurement is not published. Hull 16.4m, beam 4.8m, draft 2.1m.
Side decks are described as sheltered and secure thanks to high bulwarks and full-length railings, with no reported accessibility complaints in owner forums. Exact deck width is not specified.

Twin windlasses and a robust, flexible roller arrangement on the bow - more capable than the Super Maramu's simpler setup. The trade-off is a narrower chain locker: Cruising Consulting reports the chain jamming the chainpipe while retrieving 100m of rode, needing to be cleared by hand, which never happened on their Super Maramu.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

facts.value: Hard sourced heavy-weather data is thin. The best on-the-water record is the Cruising World sail trial in 16-knot beam winds and choppy cross seas off Florida, where the boat logged an effortless 9 knots - a moderate test, not a storm. Brokerage and community material notes the boat has completed full circumnavigations and is 'tested in every ocean,' and that under the inner-forestay staysail it balances well as the wind builds, but no detailed first-hand gale, knockdown or survival-storm narrative appears in the sourced record.

Designed from the outset with an inner forestay carrying a self-tacking staysail - the single biggest rig difference from the Super Maramu and the boat's main heavy-weather tool. As the wind builds the crew rolls away the genoa and balances the boat on the mizzen and staysail. The ketch rig multiplies the reefing options, letting a short-handed couple shorten down in stages without leaving the cockpit.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting
Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - fetchinketch

facts.value: The whole sail plan is electric-furling - main, genoa and staysail - and this is the boat's signature maintenance burden. The Bamar genoa and staysail furlers were so problematic that Bamar offered discounted redesigned units; owners report repeated electric furler motor and gearbox failures, with one citing the third or fourth set of furler motors in 13 years. The mainsail furler motor and gearbox is likewise a known wear item. Owners variously rebuild, replace with later Bamar units (such as the MEJ 2.02 genoa furler) or retrofit Reckmann or Harken electric systems. A furler failure at sea on a roller-furling-dependent ketch is a serious passage hazard, so this system is the first thing to inspect and budget for.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting
Amel Yacht Owners group - electric furler / main furler threads
Reckmann electric furler retrofit for Amel yachts

facts.value: Standing rigging on a ketch has a finite life and should be inspected on every older boat; replacement is typically considered at 15-20 years. Owner reports on the Amel Yacht Owners group put a full re-rig of a 54 at roughly 10,000 USD, plus about 6,000 USD if adding or upgrading the staysail rig and around 2,000 USD for a new staysail. The two-mast rig increases scope versus a sloop.

sources.title: Amel Yacht Owners group - Standing rigging on 54
Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

facts.value: Three issues dominate: the Bamar electric furlers (genoa, staysail and main) with repeated motor and gearbox failures; the Volvo D3 engine's early-electronic reliability reputation; and the optional hydraulic passerelle's mechanical and corrosion failures. The narrow chain locker can jam the chainpipe. Flush deck hatches should be checked for leaks, and the monolithic hull-to-deck bond inspected. Amel's all-systems philosophy means everything is custom and the sheer count of powered systems is the real ownership cost.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht
Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

facts.value: Amel School estimates annual operating cost at roughly 10-15% of the yacht's value, with the owner's own technical skill the single biggest variable. The same source warns that a cheap Amel is almost always a boat needing a significant and costly refit, because deferred maintenance on this many systems compounds quickly.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

facts.value: Listed new at about 965,000 USD in 2007. Today a well-maintained Amel 54 typically asks roughly 400,000-650,000 USD (one 2009 example listed at 440,000 euros including VAT). Condition and refit history drive the spread more than year does.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

facts.value: Volvo Penta D3 parts and Volvo's worldwide dealer network support the engine, though electronic diagnosis needs a Volvo VODIA tester rather than a generic shop. Amel-specific parts come through official suppliers and the very active 1,400-plus-member Amel Yacht Owners group, which holds drawings and workarounds for discontinued parts. Bamar furler parts are available but can be expensive, and Reckmann offers a plug-and-play electric-furler retrofit for Amel models. The AUTOPROP propeller is serviceable through its maker.

sources.title: Reckmann electric furler retrofit for Amel yachts

praise: Ultimate short-handed cruiser - exceptionally well set up for two-person sailing in all weather
One of the easiest boats of this size to sail single-handed thanks to the cockpit and deck layout
Very comfortable and very well-finished liveaboard design
Land Rover of sailboats - rugged, reliable cruiser with fully integrated systems
Dishwasher, washer/dryer and reversible air conditioning all standard
Enclosed, heated doghouse helm lets the crew run the boat from shelter in foul weather
Four watertight bulkheads and high bulwarks with full-length railings give real peace of mind
Inner-forestay staysail balances the ketch beautifully as the wind builds
Three cold-storage units, 900L water plus a watermaker, and 900L fuel make it genuinely self-sufficient
Strong resale value; owners tend to keep them long-term or move up within the brand
More Amels have circumnavigated than almost any other production boat; the 54 is proven in every ocean
complaints: Electric furlers (Bamar genoa, staysail and main) are a repeated and expensive failure point
Volvo D3 engine has an early-electronic reliability reputation and needs a Volvo VODIA tester to diagnose
Optional hydraulic passerelle is notorious for mechanical and corrosion failures
Narrow chain locker can jam the chainpipe when retrieving the rode
Not fast for its size - a heavy, moderately canvassed cruiser, not a performance boat
Enclosed helm behind a windshield gives shelter at the cost of visibility from the chair
Everything is custom Amel with little owner ability to change the design
The sheer number of powered systems is the largest maintenance burden; a cheap Amel usually needs a costly refit
known_issues: Bamar genoa and staysail electric furlers - repeated motor and gearbox failures; Bamar offered discounted redesigned units
Mainsail furler motor and gearbox is a recurring wear item; some owners are on their third or fourth furler set
Volvo D3 electronic faults - boost-sensor failure, 1500 rpm limp mode, VODIA-only diagnosis
Optional hydraulic passerelle - mechanical and corrosion failures
Narrow chain locker jamming the chainpipe on retrieval
Flush deck hatches should be checked for leaks; monolithic hull-to-deck bond worth inspecting
Standing rigging has a finite life and should be inspected on older boats

forums.title: Amel Yacht Owners - electric furler for Amel 54 thread
Sailing Anarchy - Amel 54 or HR 46 Discussion

refit_items.system: Electric furlers (Bamar genoa, staysail and mainsail)
refit_items.action: Rebuild failed furler motors/gearboxes, replace with later Bamar units, or retrofit a Reckmann or Harken electric system. Budget all three furlers on an older boat given the documented repeat-failure history; a roughly 8,000 USD headsail-furler retrofit is a typical rigger budget figure, and a full multi-furler overhaul runs well above that.

sources.title: Amel Yacht Owners group - electric furler threads
Reckmann electric furler retrofit for Amel yachts

refit_items.system: Standing rigging (both masts, shrouds, stays, inner forestay)
refit_items.action: Full replacement on a boat over 15-20 years or showing fatigue/corrosion. Owner reports put a 54 re-rig near 10,000 USD, plus about 6,000 USD to add or upgrade the staysail rig and around 2,000 USD for a new staysail. Two-mast scope adds cost over a sloop.

sources.title: Amel Yacht Owners group - Standing rigging on 54

refit_items.action: Diesel-specialist survey with VODIA read of the ECU before purchase. Routine service (impeller, belts, filters, fuel system) at the low end; sensor/injector work, raw-water pump, heat exchanger and transmission service at the high end. Full repower not normally needed but factor the D3's electronic-reliability reputation.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht
Volvo D3 110 hp - Problems? | YBW Forum

refit_items.action: Rebuild, replace, or remove altogether - mechanical wear and corrosion are near-universal complaints. Many owners simply remove it.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

refit_items.action: Replace the 24V house bank (AGM or LiFePO4) on the 6-10 year interval; lithium at the high end. The heavy hotel load (three AC units, dual refrigeration, watermaker, electric furlers/winches) makes a large, well-managed bank essential.
Add a hard-top or rail-mounted solar array (typically 600-1200W) to offset the large 24V hotel load and reduce generator hours. Not factory-standard on most hulls.
Compressor service or replacement, refrigerant recharge and leak testing across the front-loading fridge and two freezers.
Membrane replacement (every 2-5 years), high-pressure pump service, pre-filter changes, feed-pump and pressure-switch service.
Refrigerant leak check, compressor service, sea-strainer cleaning and condensate-drain inspection. Continuous tropical running accelerates compressor wear.
Burner inspection, glow-plug replacement, heat-exchanger descaling and seal replacement.

refit_items.system: Electronics suite (chartplotter, radar, AIS, VHF, autopilots)
refit_items.action: Selective upgrade (chartplotter, AIS, VHF) at the low end; full suite including radar, instruments and autopilot drives at the high end. Furuno Navpilot 511 control heads may need replacement on aged boats.
Full four-sail replacement at the high end; main plus two headsails at the low end. Furling sails require measured replacements; no spinnaker is standard.

sources.title: Amel Yacht Owners group - rig and sails discussion

refit_items.system: Hull and deck structural survey and blister repair
refit_items.action: Survey of the single-skin hull for osmotic blistering, plus a hull-to-deck bond integrity check per Amel School guidance. Survey only at the low end; full peel-and-barrier-coat remediation at the high end.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

refit_items.action: Annual haul-out, bottom paint, anode replacement and propeller service.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furler motor / gearbox set and full Bamar bearing and seal kit (genoa, staysail and main)
provisioning_specific.why: The whole sail plan is electric-furling and these are the boat's most documented failure point - owners report being on their third or fourth furler set. A furler that won't roll in or out on a furling-dependent ketch is a passage emergency, so carry a full bearing/seal kit and ideally a spare motor.

provisioning_specific.item: Reckmann or Harken electric-furler retrofit budget (pre-passage)
provisioning_specific.why: If the original Bamar furlers are tired, retrofit a Reckmann plug-and-play (available for Amel models) or a Harken electric system before a long offshore passage rather than risk a failure mid-ocean. Riggers budget roughly 8,000 USD per headsail furler.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo D3 service kit (impeller, belts, fuel/oil filters, boost sensor) plus VODIA diagnostic access
provisioning_specific.why: The D3 is an early fully-electronic engine and its faults are electronic - boost-sensor failure and limp-mode codes. Carry the common-wear sensors and filters, and know where you can reach a VODIA tester, because a generic diesel mechanic cannot read the ECU.

provisioning_specific.item: Dessalator DUO spare membrane set and pre-filter cartridges (12-month supply)
provisioning_specific.why: The watermaker is standard and the specific Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit is confirmed. Membranes degrade faster in warm tropical water and with biological fouling; carry a spare set for passages far from chandleries.

provisioning_specific.item: 24V battery monitor and spare cells/modules sized for the AC + refrigeration + watermaker load
provisioning_specific.why: Three AC units, two freezers, a fridge, the watermaker and the electric furlers and winches are all 24V loads. The bank works hard; a good monitor and at least one spare cell cluster protect the boat's autonomy on long passages.

provisioning_specific.item: AC raw-water strainer baskets (three sets) and seacock service kit
provisioning_specific.why: All three AC units use raw-seawater cooling and clog with algae and jellyfish in tropical anchorages. The watertight bulkheads make seacock access awkward, so carry spare strainer baskets and a seacock grease/handle kit.

provisioning_specific.item: Inner-forestay staysail halyard, furling line and sheet spares
provisioning_specific.why: The staysail on the inner forestay is the boat's primary heavy-weather sail and sees its highest loads exactly when you most need it. Carry spares for the halyard, furling line and sheets and inspect them before each offshore passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto / Espar heater glow-plug and combustion-tube kit
provisioning_specific.why: The 5000W diesel heater is standard and its glow plugs and combustion tubes are the highest-wear, model-specific consumables. Essential for cold-weather passages to high latitudes.

provisioning_specific.item: AUTOPROP service parts and Amel-specific 24V fuses, breakers and wiring diagrams
provisioning_specific.why: Everything on the boat is custom Amel and Cruising World notes electronics and wiring routed through the watertight bulkheads are hard to access. The original wiring diagrams plus a full assortment of 24V fuses and breakers turn a no-spares-in-stock situation into a fixable one offshore.

gaps: stability.stix - No STIX rating located in any source; not commonly published for cruising vessels
stability.avs - No Angle of Vanishing Stability (AVS/LPS) or righting-moment curve located; Amel does not publish detailed stability curves
tropical.shade - No information on cabin shade systems (roller blinds, solar control, awnings)
ergonomics.mob_recovery - No dedicated MOB recovery system or procedure documented for the Amel 54 specifically
ergonomics.freeboard - Exact freeboard measurement not published
ergonomics.side_decks - Exact deck width and walkway dimensions not specified
self_steering.electrical_autonomy - Factory battery capacity varies by hull; the 1,320 Ah figure is from one well-refitted example, not a standard spec
heavy_weather.storm_report - No detailed first-hand gale, knockdown or survival-storm narrative in sourced material; the Cruising World test was moderate (16 knots)
cold.watertight_bulkheads.location - Exact placement of the four bulkheads not detailed
refit.furler.exact_cost - Furler overhaul costs vary widely by chosen solution (rebuild vs Bamar vs Reckmann/Harken retrofit); no single Amel-54 quote confirmed
engine.exact_volvo_variant - Sources confirm the Volvo D3-110 family but the precise D3 sub-variant (D3-110, D3-110i) per hull is not consistently stated

cleaned_facts.value: Class A (EU Design Category A) - certified for extended oceanic voyages; boat-specs.com defines Category A as wind up to Force 9 and waves up to 10m. The category certifies the boat for conditions up to that maximum, not for conditions exceeding Force 8 / 4m as an earlier description implied.

cleaned_facts.value: 1.9, below the 2.0 ocean-passage threshold, confirmed by sailboat-cruising.com and boat-specs.com. A favourable indicator, though not a measured stability metric.

This is now sourced data rather than an unknown - the boat is moderately canvassed and heavy, easily driven offshore but unhurried in light air.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance Indicators

cleaned_facts.value: Volvo Penta D3, 110 hp, confirmed as the 54's engine by Amel School and Cruising Consulting. Brokerage sheets list it generically as a '110 hp Volvo TurboDiesel.' This corrects earlier records that named a mechanical TAMD-series engine, which belonged to the older Super Maramu, not the 54.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

cleaned_facts.value: The Volvo D3 is the most-criticised system on the 54. Amel School: 'a reputation for being less reliable than other power plants used by the yard.' Cruising Consulting: an early fully-electronic engine that has been 'a source of trouble for some owners,' improved by later Volvo updates. Faults are electronic (boost sensor, 1500 rpm limp mode, VODIA-only diagnosis). A diesel-specialist survey is essential.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

cleaned_facts.value: Webasto 24V/5000W diesel heater confirmed on the 2009 Charpentier listing; a 2010 listing specifies an Espar/Eberspacher 5000W unit, so brand varied by hull. The heated, enclosed doghouse is the real cold-weather feature.
Balsa-cored fiberglass/polyester sandwich deck over a single-skin fiberglass/polyester hull, confirmed by boat-specs.com. No source addresses insulation effectiveness or cold-weather comfort.
Deck-stepped masts confirmed by boat-specs.com ('deck-stepped mast with 2 levels of swept-back spreaders'). Both masts stepped on deck; no cabin cold-bridge.
Four watertight bulkheads confirmed by sailboatdata and the Cruising World review. A core Amel collision/grounding safety feature.

sources.title: The Amel 54 Sailboat Specs & Key Performance Indicators

cleaned_facts.value: Enclosed doghouse helm confirmed by Cruising World: push-button controls and readouts for almost all systems, behind a hard windshield, with visibility from the chair limited. The defining Amel design trait.

Roughly six fans distributed through the boat per the 2009 listing, plus opening hatches and portlights. No source confirms a specific hatch count. Flush deck hatches warrant a leak check per Amel School.
Three separate cold-storage units confirmed by the 2009 listing and Cruising World: front-loading 24V fridge, top-loading 24V galley freezer, and a second freezer under the saloon settee.
Watermaker is standard equipment; the 2009 example carries a Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit. Generic reverse-osmosis pressure and yield figures are not confirmed in available sources.

Three reversible air-conditioning units (aft cabin, saloon, forward cabin) confirmed standard by both the 2009 and 2010 Charpentier listings. A heavy electrical and seawater-cooling load that shapes generator and battery sizing.
Hydrovane confirmed as an independent vane-to-auxiliary-rudder system, and the 2009 listing confirms one Amel 54 was fitted with one as a retrofit. Not factory standard.
Two Furuno Navpilot 511 units confirmed on the 2009 example - full short-handed redundancy. Equipment on a specific vessel rather than a guaranteed fit on every hull.

24V house system. The 2009 example carries an Onan 11 kVA generator and a 12 x 110Ah (1,320 Ah) 24V bank with Mastervolt charger and 2,500W inverter. This is one well-refitted boat's spec, not a guaranteed factory figure, but it shows the scale of bank the hotel load demands.

Twin windlasses and a robust roller arrangement confirmed by Cruising Consulting, which also reports the narrower chain locker jamming the chainpipe while retrieving 100m of rode - an issue absent on their Super Maramu.

The boat has completed full circumnavigations per brokerage/community material, and balances well on the inner-forestay staysail as wind builds, but no detailed first-hand gale or knockdown account survives in the sourced record.

Inner forestay carrying a staysail confirmed as a designed-in feature by both fetchinketch.net and cruisingconsulting.com, and as the largest rig difference from the Super Maramu. Improves heavy-weather balance; the ketch rig multiplies reefing combinations for short-handed crews.

Bamar genoa and staysail furlers were 'very problematic, to the point that Bamar offered significant discounts for redesigned units' (Cruising Consulting). Owners on the Amel Yacht Owners group report repeated electric furler motor/gearbox failures - one citing the third or fourth furler set in 13 years - across the main as well as the headsails. Solutions range from rebuilds to later Bamar units (e.g. MEJ 2.02) to Reckmann/Harken retrofits.

These are owner-quoted figures for this boat size, more reliable than earlier estimates extrapolated from much smaller boats.

sources.title: Amel Yacht Owners group - Standing rigging on 54

cleaned_facts.value: Bamar electric furlers (genoa, staysail, main) with repeated failures; Volvo D3 early-electronic reliability reputation; hydraulic passerelle corrosion/mechanical failure; narrow chain locker jamming. Flush deck hatches and the monolithic hull-to-deck bond warrant survey attention. Confirmed by Cruising Consulting and Amel School.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting
Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

cleaned_facts.value: Amel School estimates annual operating cost at roughly 10-15% of yacht value, owner skill the biggest variable, and warns a cheap Amel almost always needs a costly refit. Consistent with the boat's many powered, custom systems.

sources.title: Amel Yacht School - Guide to Acquiring an Amel Yacht

cleaned_facts.value: New about 965,000 USD in 2007 (Cruising World). Today roughly 400,000-650,000 USD for well-maintained boats, with one 2009 example at 440,000 euros incl. VAT. Condition and refit history drive the spread more than build year.

sources.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu - Cruising Consulting

cleaned_facts.value: Volvo D3 supported by Volvo's dealer network (electronic diagnosis needs a VODIA tester). Amel-specific parts and discontinued-part workarounds flow through the 1,400-plus-member Amel Yacht Owners group. Bamar furler parts are available but pricey; Reckmann offers a plug-and-play Amel furler retrofit. This corrects an earlier record that cited FYB Marine for a TAMD-series engine the 54 does not use.

sources.title: Reckmann electric furler retrofit for Amel yachts

removed.reason: Earlier records identified the engine as a Volvo TAMD41 (a mechanical engine) and cited FYB Marine and Heavy Duty Kits for parts. Model-specific sources (Amel School, Cruising Consulting) confirm the Amel 54 used the Volvo D3 - an early fully-electronic engine - and that the TAMD belonged to the older Super Maramu. The TAMD41 identification and its parts citations are removed as a material factual error.
The '55 bar / 800 psi / 20% freshwater yield' figures were generic RO industry parameters, not Amel-54-specific. Removed; replaced with the confirmed Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit.

The Amel 50 Yachting World review was previously cited as evidence of Amel 54 cold-weather heating. That review covers a different model and mentions no Webasto heater. Cross-model attribution removed; heater confirmed instead from the 2009/2010 Charpentier listings for the 54 itself.

fabrication_flags: Earlier records named a Volvo TAMD41 (mechanical) engine for the Amel 54 and cited FYB Marine / Heavy Duty Kits for its parts. The 54 actually uses the Volvo D3 (early fully-electronic); the TAMD belonged to the Super Maramu. This was a material engine misidentification, now corrected.

Earlier 'inferred' battery capacity derived from fuel/water/ballast figures had no logical basis. Replaced with the actual 12 x 110Ah bank documented on the 2009 example, clearly flagged as one boat's spec rather than a universal factory figure.
Generic RO watermaker pressure/yield numbers were presented as if Amel-54-specific. Removed in favour of the confirmed Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit.
verify.verdict: The Amel 54 (Chantiers Amel, ~2005-2011, ~50 hulls) is a heavily built, fully-systemed bluewater ketch and a benchmark short-handed circumnavigator. Core specifications are well confirmed by multiple independent sources: EU Design Category A, capsize screening 1.9, ballast ratio 29%, displacement ~17,500 kg, 140 m² sail area (SA/D ~21-22), L-keel with bulb on a skeg-hung rudder, deck-stepped ketch rig with an inner-forestay staysail, four watertight bulkheads, an enclosed doghouse helm, three reversible AC units, dual Furuno autopilots, a Dessalator watermaker, and triple refrigeration. This enrichment corrects a material error in earlier records - the engine is the Volvo D3 (an early fully-electronic diesel widely flagged as the 54's weakest choice), not the mechanical TAMD41 of the Super Maramu - and adds the boat's defining ownership reality: the all-electric furling rig (Bamar genoa, staysail and main) is a repeat failure point, with owners reporting multiple furler-motor/gearbox replacements and turning to Reckmann/Harken retrofits. Heavy-weather evidence remains limited to a moderate Cruising World trial plus general circumnavigation reputation; no detailed first-hand storm narrative is in the sourced record, so the storm verdict stays uncited. Standing-rigging and furler costs are now grounded in Amel-owner figures rather than extrapolations from far smaller boats. Confidence is raised to 0.78 to reflect the corrected engine, sourced spec/ratio data, and owner-grounded cost figures, while keeping honest uncertainty on stability curves, freeboard, and storm behaviour.

cold_score.rationale: The Amel 54 is one of the best-protected boats in this guide for cold and foul-weather sailing. Its standout feature is the fully enclosed doghouse helm, where the crew steers and runs every system from a seated position behind a hard windshield - confirmed by Cruising World. A standard 5000W diesel heater (Webasto on some hulls, Espar on others) warms that same space, and four watertight bulkheads add collision and flooding protection. The masts are deck-stepped, so unlike a keel-stepped boat there is no cold-bridge drawing heat out of the cabin. The score is held back from the top tier because no source quantifies hull or cabin insulation, and cold-climate water and tankage details are undocumented rather than confirmed - so the rating leans on the strong, sourced shelter-and-heating features while staying honest about the unquantified insulation.
cold_score.basis: Heated, fully enclosed doghouse helm and a standard 5000W diesel heater are the core strengths; deck-stepped masts avoid a cabin cold-bridge and four watertight bulkheads add safety; held below the top tier because insulation and cold-weather tankage are undocumented.

tropic_score.rationale: Strongly equipped for the tropics. Three separate cold-storage units (front-loading fridge plus two freezers) and three reversible air-conditioning units are confirmed standard, and a Dessalator watermaker plus 900 litres of tankage make the boat genuinely self-sufficient for water and food on long warm-water passages. Ventilation is adequate - opening hatches, portlights and roughly six fans - though it relies more on AC than on passive airflow, and the AC load is heavy on the electrical system. The boat is moderately canvassed for its weight (sail-area-to-displacement around 21-22, now confirmed from sources rather than unknown), so it is comfortable but unhurried in light tropical air. Cabin shade systems are undocumented. The rating reflects excellent confirmed cooling, refrigeration and water self-sufficiency, tempered by AC-dependent ventilation and only moderate light-air sail power.
tropic_score.basis: Confirmed standard triple refrigeration, three reversible AC units, and a Dessalator watermaker with 900L tankage drive a high score; tempered by AC-dependent (rather than passive) ventilation, undocumented cabin shade, and only moderate light-air sail power.

stability_score.rationale: No angle of vanishing stability or righting-moment curve is published for the Amel 54 - Amel does not release detailed stability data - so the score rests on the EU Design Category A certification, which two independent sources confirm and which qualifies the boat for the most demanding offshore conditions (winds up to Force 9, seas up to 10m). Category A maps to a strong stability rating. The supporting indicators all point the same way without being measured stability metrics: a capsize screening figure of 1.9 (below the 2.0 ocean threshold), a 29% ballast ratio on a low bulb keel, a skeg-hung rudder, and four watertight bulkheads. The score reflects confirmed Category A certification, with the honest caveat that no published stability curve exists to verify the angle directly.
stability_score.basis: Scored on confirmed EU Design Category A certification because no AVS or righting-moment curve is published; capsize screening 1.9, 29% ballast on a bulb keel, skeg rudder and four watertight bulkheads are corroborating but non-measured indicators.

storm_verdict.text: The sourced record does not support a firm storm verdict. The only on-the-water performance account in the verified facts is a Cruising World sail trial in 16-knot beam winds and choppy cross seas off Florida, where the boat logged an effortless 9 knots - the facts themselves classify this as a moderate test, not a storm. No first-hand owner accounts of gale-force or storm-force sailing, no knockdown reports, and no survival-storm narratives appear in any cited source. What is confirmed is the design intent: the inner-forestay staysail is a built-in heavy-weather tool that balances the ketch as the wind builds, the ketch rig gives a short-handed crew many staged reefing options, and the boat holds EU Design Category A certification for conditions up to Force 9 and 10m seas. Amels in general, and the 54 specifically, have completed circumnavigations and are widely described as tested in every ocean. But certification and reputation are not a measured storm account, so any specific claim about the 54's behaviour in breaking seas or sustained gales would go beyond the sourced evidence.

refit_projection.assumptions: The Amel 54 was built from about 2005 to 2011, so a boat bought today is roughly 15 to 21 years old and every age-triggered system should be treated as due. The single most important budget line is the all-electric furling rig: the Bamar genoa, staysail and mainsail furlers are a documented repeat failure point, and a buyer should plan to rebuild or retrofit them rather than hope. The engine is the Volvo D3 (an early fully-electronic diesel with a mixed reliability reputation, not the mechanical TAMD of the older Super Maramu), so the engine line covers a thorough diesel-specialist survey and sensor/injector work rather than a repower, which is rarely needed. Standing-rigging and staysail-rig costs come from Amel owners' own figures for this boat size rather than from far smaller boats. The optional hydraulic passerelle is budgeted because corrosion and mechanical failure are near-universal complaints. The large 24V hotel load (three AC units, triple refrigeration, watermaker, electric furlers and winches) drives the battery line and supports an optional solar addition to cut generator hours. All figures are our estimates; few line items have an Amel-54-specific quote, and ranges reflect labour-rate differences between the Caribbean, the Mediterranean and the Pacific. Low estimates assume a sound survey; high estimates assume several systems come due at once, including a full furler retrofit and lithium upgrade.

by_item.system: Electric furlers (Bamar genoa, staysail and mainsail)
by_item.note: The boat's signature failure point. Rebuild at the low end; a full multi-furler retrofit to Reckmann or Harken at the high end. Riggers budget around 8,000 USD per headsail furler; owners report being on their third or fourth furler set. Inspect first, budget regardless.

by_item.system: Standing rigging (both masts) and staysail rig
by_item.note: Owner-quoted: roughly 10,000 USD for a 54 re-rig, plus about 6,000 USD to add or upgrade the staysail rig and around 2,000 USD for a new staysail. Two-mast ketch scope exceeds a sloop.

Diesel-specialist survey with a VODIA ECU read is essential given the D3's early-electronic reputation. Routine service at the low end; sensor/injector work, raw-water pump, heat exchanger and transmission service at the high end. Repower rarely needed.
Rebuild, replace or remove - mechanical and corrosion failures are near-universal complaints. Some owners simply delete it.

AGM replacement at the low end; LiFePO4 upgrade at the high end. The heavy hotel load (three AC units, triple refrigeration, watermaker, electric furlers/winches) demands a large, well-managed bank. The 1,320 Ah figure on one refitted example shows the scale.
600-1200W hard-top or rail array to offset the 24V hotel load and cut generator hours. Not factory-standard on most hulls.
Refrigerant leak check, compressor service, sea-strainer cleaning, condensate-drain inspection. Continuous tropical running accelerates compressor wear.
Compressor service or replacement, refrigerant recharge and leak testing across the fridge and two freezers.
Membrane replacement every 2-5 years; high-pressure pump and feed-pump service; pre-filter changes. Unit confirmed on the 2009 example.
Burner inspection, glow-plug replacement, heat-exchanger descaling, seal replacement. Unit confirmed on the 2009 and 2010 examples.
Selective upgrade at the low end; full suite including radar, instruments and autopilot drives at the high end. Furuno Navpilot 511 control heads may need replacement on aged boats.
Furling main and headsail recut/replacement at the low end; full four-sail replacement at the high end. No spinnaker is standard.

by_item.system: Hull and deck structural survey and blister repair
by_item.note: Single-skin hull osmotic-blister survey plus a hull-to-deck bond integrity check per Amel School. Survey only at the low end; peel-and-barrier-coat remediation at the high end.
Multiple through-hulls for AC cooling, watermaker, engine and heads. Access is awkward around the watertight bulkheads. Older hulls may need a full set replaced.
Liferaft recertification or replacement, EPIRB battery and registration, flares. Interval-based regardless of boat age.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare furler motor/gearbox and full Bamar bearing and seal set (genoa, staysail and main)
provisioning_specific.why: The all-electric furling rig is the most-documented failure point on the 54, with owners reporting their third or fourth furler set. A furler stuck open or shut on a furling-dependent ketch is a passage emergency. Carry a full bearing/seal kit and, ideally, a spare motor.

provisioning_specific.item: Reckmann or Harken electric-furler retrofit budget (pre-passage)
provisioning_specific.why: If the Bamar furlers are tired, retrofit a Reckmann plug-and-play (available for Amel models) or a Harken system before a long passage rather than risk a mid-ocean failure. Riggers budget roughly 8,000 USD per headsail furler.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo D3 service kit (impeller, belts, filters, boost sensor) and a plan for VODIA access
provisioning_specific.why: The D3 is an early fully-electronic engine whose faults are electronic - boost-sensor failure and limp-mode codes. Carry the common-wear sensors and filters, and know where a VODIA tester is reachable, because a generic mechanic cannot read the ECU.

provisioning_specific.item: Dessalator DUO spare membrane set and pre-filter cartridges (12-month supply)
provisioning_specific.why: The watermaker is standard and the Dessalator DUO 100 L/H unit is confirmed. Membranes degrade faster in warm tropical water; carry a spare set for passages far from chandleries. With 900L tankage plus the watermaker, the boat is self-sufficient for water.

provisioning_specific.item: 24V battery monitor and spare cells sized for the AC + refrigeration + watermaker load
provisioning_specific.why: Three AC units, two freezers, a fridge, the watermaker and the electric furlers/winches are all 24V loads. The bank works hard; a good monitor and a spare cell cluster protect the boat's autonomy on long passages.

provisioning_specific.item: AC raw-water strainer baskets (three sets) and seacock service kit
provisioning_specific.why: All three AC units use raw-seawater cooling and clog with algae and jellyfish in tropical anchorages. The watertight bulkheads make seacock access awkward, so carry spare strainer baskets and a seacock grease/handle kit.

provisioning_specific.item: Inner-forestay staysail halyard, furling line and sheet spares
provisioning_specific.why: The staysail on the inner forestay is the boat's primary heavy-weather sail and sees its highest loads exactly when you most need it. Carry spares for its halyard, furling line and sheets and inspect them before each offshore passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto / Espar heater glow-plug and combustion-tube kit
provisioning_specific.why: The 5000W diesel heater is standard, and its glow plugs and combustion tubes are the highest-wear, model-specific consumables. Essential for cold-weather passages to high latitudes.

provisioning_specific.item: Amel-specific 24V fuses, breakers and the original wiring diagram set
provisioning_specific.why: Everything aboard is custom Amel, and Cruising World notes electronics and wiring routed through the watertight bulkheads are hard to access. The original diagrams plus a full assortment of 24V fuses and breakers turn a no-spares-in-stock situation into a fixable one offshore.

field_reports.title: Effortless 9 knots off Florida in chop
field_reports.account: In a Cruising World sea trial, the Amel 54 logged an effortless 9 knots in 16 knots of wind on the beam with choppy cross seas. The conservative ketch sail plan and full-skeg rudder gave a composed, armchair-style ride rather than a sporty one, consistent with Amel's comfort-first design brief.
field_reports.conditions: 16 knots true on the beam, choppy cross seas

field_reports.title: One person can run the whole boat
field_reports.account: Cruising World's review stressed the Amel 54's shorthanded ergonomics for older cruising couples: electric furling main and genoa, electric winches, a bow thruster and push-button controls at the steering station, all operable from the protected center cockpit. US agent Joel Potter summarized it as 'If you can lift 50 pounds, you can do everything on this boat yourself.' Standard liveaboard kit included a dishwasher, washer/dryer and deep freeze.

field_reports.title: Carango circumnavigates via the World ARC
field_reports.account: Owners Peter and Vicky Forbes sailed their Amel 54 Carango on the World ARC circumnavigation, one of two 54s aiming to complete the full loop. Forbes told Yachting World he chose the boat primarily for its inherent safety features and cockpit-controlled sail plan, valuing comfort and safety over outright speed, and noted the ketch rig's shorter masts and smaller sails suited a crew 'of a certain age.' The first leg from St Lucia to Colombia involved over 40 hours of motoring in light air, yet he still called it some of his best sailing since buying her in 2013.
field_reports.conditions: Light air on the St Lucia–Colombia leg; 40+ hours motoring

source.title: World ARC crews talk to us in Colombia — Yachting World

field_reports.title: Holds firm in 45-knot Tuamotu squall
field_reports.account: On their Pacific leg toward Rangiroa, the Forbeses' Amel 54 Carango rode out a sudden storm with 45-knot winds and high waves inside an atoll lagoon. Several nearby boats dragged anchor while Carango held firm, which the owners credited to the boat's ground tackle and the security of the heavy, well-equipped Amel package.
field_reports.conditions: 45-knot winds, high waves in a lagoon

source.title: Carango Amel 54 #035 — In full Sail (owner blog)

field_reports.title: Beats an Oyster 62 to windward off Brazil
field_reports.account: Working north along the Brazilian coast toward Fernando de Noronha and Grenada, owner Peter Forbes reported Carango going well close-hauled on starboard tack with barber haulers and running backstays rigged on the staysail and a partial yankee, main and mizzen sheeted in hard. He wrote that 'Carango goes very well to windward and we have held off challenge from the 62 foot Oyster — very satisfactory,' and that the boat handled a two-person watch system on long passages.

source.title: Carango Amel 54 #035 — Up further North in Brazil (owner blog)

field_reports.title: Engine failure and 'appalling' Amel support
field_reports.account: After a main-engine failure on a reef, Carango was towed into Papeete, where the owners juggled a broken anchor windlass and a leaking generator anti-siphon valve at the same time. Forbes found the local Volvo agent unreliable and emailed contacts in New Zealand, Australia and France to source help, venting in the blog about what he called Amel's 'appalling' factory support — a candid counterpoint to the brand's reliability reputation.

source.title: Carango Amel 54 #035 — Volvo Engine disaster (owner blog)

field_reports.title: Owner blog flags furler and chain-handling quirks
field_reports.account: Amel owner Bill Kinney (who sails the Super Maramu Harmonie) compared the 54 to the older boat after a week aboard. He praised the 54's inner-forestay staysail for balancing the boat nicely in heavy weather, but criticized its Bamar genoa and staysail furlers as less robust than Amel's own design, reported that 100m of chain jammed the 54's chainpipe, and noted the hydraulic passerelle's reputation for constant mechanical problems.

source.title: Compare and Contrast: The Amel 54 vs. the Super Maramu — fetchinketch (owner blog)
